---
title: "Lab 07a: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) & SVD"
subtitle: "STT843: Multivariate Data Analysis"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
```

## 1. Singular Value Decomposition (SVD)

The lecture defined SVD as $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{U}\mathbf{\Sigma}\mathbf{V}^\top$. In R, we use the svd() function.Note: We must center the data first, as PCA depends on the covariance structure around the mean.

```r
# Manual Centering
X_centered <- scale(X, center = TRUE, scale = FALSE)

# Compute SVD
X_svd <- svd(X_centered)

# U: Left singular vectors (related to scores)
# D: Singular values (diagonal of Sigma)
# V: Right singular vectors (Loadings/Principal Directions)
V <- X_svd$v
colnames(V) <- c("PC1", "PC2")
V
```

The first column of $V$ is our "shark head tilt"—the direction of maximum spread.

## 2. Principal Components via Covariance

Alternatively, we can find the components using the eigenvalues of the sample covariance matrix $\mathbf{S} = \frac{1}{n-1}\mathbf{X}^\top\mathbf{X}$.

```r
S <- cov(X_centered)
eigen_dec <- eigen(S)

# Eigenvectors match the V matrix from SVD
eigen_dec$vectors
```

## 3. PCA in Practice: prcomp

While SVD and eigen show the math, the most common way to run PCA in R is prcomp. It automatically handles centering.

```r
pca_res <- prcomp(X, center = TRUE, scale. = FALSE)

# Summary shows Proportion of Variance
summary(pca_res)

# Plot the PC directions on the original data
plot(X_centered, asp = 1, pch = 19, col = "grey")
abline(0, V[2,1]/V[1,1], col = "red", lwd = 2) # PC1
abline(0, V[2,2]/V[1,2], col = "blue", lwd = 2) # PC2
legend("topleft", legend=c("PC1 (Head Tilt)", "PC2"), col=c("red", "blue"), lwd=2)
```

## 4. The Effect of Scaling (Scale Invariance)
As noted in the lecture, PCA is NOT scale-invariant. Let's see what happens if we change the units of one variable (e.g., from meters to centimeters).

``` r
# Multiply X2 by 100
X_scaled <- X
X_scaled[,2] <- X_scaled[,2] * 100

# PCA on unscaled data
pca_unscaled <- prcomp(X_scaled, scale. = FALSE)
# PCA on standardized data (Correlation Matrix)
pca_standardized <- prcomp(X_scaled, scale. = TRUE)

print("Loadings without standardization (Dominated by X2):")
pca_unscaled$rotation[,1]

print("Loadings with standardization (Balanced):")
pca_standardized$rotation[,1]
```

## iris data example ##
First, we isolate the numeric measurements. In PCA, we typically standardize ($scale = TRUE$) because the variables have different ranges (e.g., Petal Length varies much more than Sepal Width).

```r
data(iris)
iris_numeric <- iris[, 1:4]

# Run PCA with scaling
iris_pca <- prcomp(iris_numeric, center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
```

# The "Loadings" (The Rotation Matrix)
The loadings ($\mathbf{V}$ or rotation) tell us how much each original variable contributes to the new axes.

```r
iris_pca$rotation
```

Interpretation: You will likely see that Petal.Length and Petal.Width have very high loadings on PC1. This means PC1 is essentially a "Petal Size" index.

The Contrast: If one variable has a positive loading and another has a negative loading on the same PC, that PC is measuring the difference or "contrast" between those two features.
 
# The "Scree Plot" (Essential Dimensions)
 How many components do we need? The Scree Plot visualizes the eigenvalues ($\lambda_i$).
 ```r
 # Plot variance explained
plot(iris_pca, type = "l", main = "Scree Plot for Iris PCA")
```
In the iris data, PC1 usually explains ~73% of the variance, and PC2 explains ~23%. Together, they account for over 95% of the total information. We can effectively discard PC3 and PC4 with almost no loss of "structure."

#The Biplot: Visualizing Scores and Loadings
The Biplot is the most powerful tool for interpretation. It plots the PC scores (the samples) and the PC loadings (the variables) on the same axes.
```r
biplot(iris_pca, cex = 0.7, col = c("gray", "red"))
```
Clusters: You will notice that Iris setosa forms a very distinct cluster far away from versicolor and virginica.

Directionality: The red arrows (loadings) pointing toward a cluster indicate that those samples have high values for those specific variables. For example, the virginica points will be pulled in the direction of the "Petal Length" arrow.

#Visualizing Species Separation
By plotting the first two PC scores, we can see how PCA (an unsupervised technique) naturally "discovers" the biological species categories.

```r
# Create a data frame for plotting
pca_data <- data.frame(iris_pca$x, Species = iris$Species)

ggplot(pca_data, aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = Species)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "PCA: Iris Species Separation",
       x = "PC1 (73% Variance)",
       y = "PC2 (23% Variance)")
```
